2. How HPE organizes its AI journey
Marketing and the product organizations—should be treated as a customer of the enterprise AI program.

Internally, we distinguish between two broad categories of AI:

— Enterprise AI, which helps our own people work differently. This includes ChatHPE, the “Adair” and “Alfred”

3. Finance & strategy: Closing the algorithmic gap
3.1 Adair: An AI assistant for investor relations
The point is not that Adair answers questions for executives, or that it replaces the craft of investor storytelling.
time for earnings cycles and a higher degree of confidence that HPE’s messages will land as intended.
AI in action at HPE: Lessons from 700+ enterprise AI initiatives  |  4

[第5页]
3.2 Alfred: Deterministic AI for performance reviews
calls “yesterday’s news.”
spreadsheet wrangling.
What makes Alfred interesting is less the interface than the engineering behind it. Finance insisted on determinism.
Gustav’s team, however, is not “build more tools.” It is “design for trust.” In finance, credibility is the real currency.
AI that cannot explain itself, or that behaves inconsistently, simply will not be used.
4. Global operations: From quote-to-cash to support
processes, supply chain, and technical support.
4.1 Conversational AI for simple service requests
One of the earliest deployments of AI in operations focused on a deceptively simple category of questions:
“Where is my order?”, “Can I have a copy of my invoice?”, and “Can you show me proof of delivery?”
Across the year, those questions represented a substantial share of HPE’s half-million order-related support cases.
Historically, they took time from support teams and often arrived outside normal office hours.
those answers around the clock, without waiting in a queue or opening a traditional case.
4.2 Smarter configuration for complex solutions
Here again, AI has become an assistant. A new recommendation engine asks a short series of targeted questions,
as well in other industries where configuration and design are complex and time-consuming.
AI in action at HPE: Lessons from 700+ enterprise AI initiatives  |  5

[第6页]
4.3 Forecasting and support at scale
Global Operations has also applied AI to the less visible, but equally critical, world of supply chain forecasting.
fewer shortages for customers.
4.4 Data as the hidden constraint
fragmented that data was across ERP systems, CRMs, sales tools, and partner platforms.
efforts in pricing analytics, renewals, and customer experience.
work, not in yet another standalone tool.
5. Marketing & sales: SDR AI and the power of a better data foundation
5.1 SDR AI: A new way to see the buyer
footprint—web visits, content downloads, event attendance, search behavior, and previous interactions with HPE.
SDRs (sales development representatives) need to turn that mass of data into a clear picture of who the buyer is,
what they care about, and whether they are ready for sales engagement.
calls, but they start from a richer, more coherent understanding of the buyer.
handled manually. Overall productivity for the team has increased by roughly a fifth.

6. Empowering the technical community
systems that power the company. This shift reflects a technical community that is learning quickly, sharing openly,
and building practical capability across the enterprise.
6.1 Tools and community
accelerate documentation, prototyping, and decision-making. These tools free engineers to focus on architecture,
problem solving, and system design. The benefit is not only speed but clarity and consistency.

[第8页]
6.2 From tools to an “Agentic Enterprise”

6.3 Key insights
Several themes define how HPE’s technical community is scaling AI responsibly and effectively.
Data context matters more than model size.
Structured evaluation accelerates responsible scaling.
performance, and operational realities.
Governance and velocity reinforce each other.
and speed.
Transparency drives adoption.
AI shifts the center of gravity from creation to curation.
The most valuable work happens when people use AI to explore possibilities and apply their expertise to refine,
shape, and validate outcomes.
AI in action at HPE: Lessons from 700+ enterprise AI initiatives  |  8

[第9页]
7. Governance, ethics, and risk: Guardrails that enable speed
partners to be credible and capable in AI.
design oversight in a world where AI systems can trigger changes in real time.
The overarching aim is not to eliminate risk—that would be impossible—but to make it visible, deliberate,
and managed.
8. Adoption, skills, and organizational change
Technology alone does not change a company. People do.
8.1 Talking honestly about work and roles
opportunities and support.
8.2 Adoption as a discipline
Successful adoption at HPE has had a few common ingredients:

— AI is integrated into the tools and workflows people already use, rather than requiring yet another portal or login.

— Managers are expected to role-model usage and encourage experimentation.

— Metrics focus on real usage and outcomes, not just the number of pilots launched.
For enterprises planning their own AI journeys, treating adoption as its own discipline—alongside architecture,
governance, and data—can make the difference between isolated wins and systemic change.
AI in action at HPE: Lessons from 700+ enterprise AI initiatives  |  9

9. From HPE’s journey to yours: A short checklist

— Are you prioritizing the right problems?
Start where the pain is clear and measurable, whether that is investor communications, support backlogs,
forecast errors, or lead conversion.

— Is your data ready for the spotlight?

— Do you have a consistent way to say “yes” and “no”?

— Are you designing for trust?

— How will you bring your people along?
10. Continuing the conversation
At HPE, we are in the midst of that process. We are using AI to reshape how we plan, operate, sell, and build,
